hints.text: Plate: black w/ **black dot** in middle (**AR only**), or white w/ blue top bar (blue bar also Brazil)
Gen3 **black car** (S. America: only AR/PE/UY); smallcam exists (AR/PE/BR)
**White-and-red chevrons** (only country in the Americas with these)
Round concrete poles (3 lines, alternating) + doubled poles; **wooden A-frame poles** (only AR in S. America)
km markers white w/ black top strip: **RN** = national, **RP** = provincial road
Default vibe: flat, treeless, sparsely populated, "wide open" & desolate
Far **west** mountainous (Andes); east rarely has mountains
**Red soil → Misiones** (Brazil-like); Chaco/Formosa = sandy soil + low bushes
**Cardón cactus** (candelabra) → NW mountains (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca, Tucumán)
Tierra del Fuego: flat grass + beech forest; Neuquén = flat w/ single poplar rows
Pampas grass → Pampas; sugarcane → Tucumán
Vineyards + mountains to the west + flat east → Mendoza wine region
Salt pans → Jujuy; red bricks (Bolivia-like) → NW (western Jujuy)
**Eucalyptus (gum) trees** everywhere, light bark; drives on the **left**
Bollard: white, **red reflector front / grey back** (NZ = red wraps around the back)
Almost all **white** road lines — yellow lines → South Africa or NZ instead
Give-way sign: white border + **black** text (NZ = red text); speed sign red circle on white rectangle (NZ = no rectangle)
Gen3 = long antenna; chevrons white-on-black & black-on-yellow
**Stobie pole** (two steel joists + concrete slab middle) is iconic to South Australia
Rusty metal poles w/ holes; **130 km/h** speed limit = NT only; termite mounds
**Yellow signposts**; black-and-white **stripy bollards** unique to WA; green-based poles
~**64% of concrete poles** are in Victoria; 'C' roads only in Victoria & Tasmania
Unique **pole-top coils** angled upward; fluorescent-border road signs; black-and-yellow bollard
**Red 'No Stopping' signs** (other states white); snorkel-fitted rare cars → NSW/ACT
Interior = **red soil**, dry; SE + SW corners greener; can "fry eggs" red outback
Tasmania: cool temperate green rolling hills (like NZ/S. Chile)
Coastal N. Queensland tropical: palms + sugarcane; Darwin (NT) dark red soil + black tree trunks
Different states use different **licence-plate** designs + slogans; 2-digit phone area codes map to state
Plate: white + **blue top stripe** (since 2018); red = commercial
**Portuguese**: -ão (not -ón), -nh (not -ñ), -lh (not -ll)
**Red soil** across most of the country (not NE, not most of Rio Grande do Sul)
"Ladder" poles: rectangular, small holes near top (also Paraguay)
Road-sign backs painted **black** (also Paraguay)
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (rest of S. America uses black-on-yellow)
Roads: double yellow centre + white outer lines; white painted kerbs common
Transparent satellite dishes (unique in Latin America); blue Fortlev roof water tanks
Gen3 car: white, stubby antenna w/ diagonal coil (also EC/CO/MX); smallcam exists (only BR/PE/AR in S. America)
Coverage mostly eastern half; Amazon very sparse
NE = Caatinga: dry soil, shrubs, succulents
Paraná pines (araucaria, flat-top branches) → south: PR, SC, RS
Carnaúba palms (fan leaves, spiked lower trunk) → CE, PI, RN
Area codes (DDD) grouped by 1st digit; ending in **1** = state capital
2-letter state acronyms on km markers / state road numbers
CEP postcode first 2 digits: numbering runs anti-clockwise from São Paulo → ends Rio Grande do Sul
Round concrete poles → south (esp. São Paulo); wooden poles → RS, Roraima, Amazonas, RJ
Open grassy plains, distant mountains, thin bunched grass
"Brown mushroom" insulators → only Pernambuco; "white mushroom" → Bahia
"Pencil pole" (smooth cone top) → Rio de Janeiro; pole street-names painted → Paraná
Speed signs say **"MAXIMUM"** (US = "SPEED LIMIT"); km/h, higher numbers
British English (centre, colour) + metric; less wordy signs than the US
**Diamond/checkerboard T-intersection sign = Canada only** (US uses rectangular)
Usually **single** yellow centre line (US = more double yellow)
Lots of **Gen2** (rural/remote) → if Gen2 in N. America, likely Canada; Gen3 mirror = two peaks (US single)
Canada Post **red** logo; communal mailboxes (rare in US, USPS = blue)
West very **mountainous** (BC, W. Alberta, Yukon); Great Plains prairies flat w/ agriculture in the centre
**Canadian Shield** (rocky outcrops + lakes + boreal forest) across the east
Area codes learnable by province; each province has its own **plate** design + flag
**Bollard**: cylindrical, 2 black bands (white or yellow band between); very common at intersections
Rural roads **numbered sequentially** (Twp Rd / Range Rd) → near-exact location from a sign
Thin, slightly curved black+white **bollard** (esp. Vancouver Island); often **concrete barriers** instead of guardrails
Bollard with a plain **black top** (also Saskatchewan); green+white striped sticker on signposts
**Bollard**: cylindrical with 2 *thick* black bands (Alberta's bands are thinner)
**Diamond** roadside markers; km markers with a white square at the bottom
**French majority**; **"Arrêt"** stop sign = QC only ("Stop/Arrêt" bilingual = New Brunswick)
White **bollards attached to guardrails** (red/green reflector); orange & white striped traffic cones
Alternating pole-top insulators (1L/2R ↔ 2L/1R); **white triangles** on signposts
Guardrail wooden posts with a **pyramid-shaped top**; diamond marker beside fire hydrants
Mainland = **tripod coverage** only (motion blur, saturated colours), no car (HK & Macau have car coverage)
**Simplified** Chinese characters (Traditional → Taiwan/HK/Macau)
Plates **blue with white text**
Coverage = small spots near east-coast cities (Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Chengdu, Yangzhou); much is inside museums / ancient cities
Plate 1st character = province indicator (京 Beijing, 沪 Shanghai, 粤 Guangdong…) — learnable
Forbidden City / Palace Museum = central Beijing; Zhoucun = only pinpointable ancient city (straight N–S street)
Museum tells the city: Hubei (Wuhan, palace grey roofs), Suzhou (white/grey angular), Long Museum (Shanghai, boxy concrete)
Long white plate + blue strip; commercial = **yellow**; **"Parrot" plates** (yellow section on the left) are unique to Denmark
Generally **flat** with wide open fields; spacious suburbs, denser urban cores
All-white lines, **double middle common**; **outer lines made of small white squares** — a great Denmark tell
Very unique **direction signs**: low to the ground, **pipe-framed**, red/white or blue/white with the arrow in the background
Street names end **-vej**; blue street sign, white text
Unique **white bollard: yellow reflector + dark-orange stripe** (also a green variant); Belgium's lacks the orange stripe
Danish letters **Æ Ø Å** (Norwegian same; Swedish uses Å Ä Ö instead)
Architecture **red brick**, orange/brown/black tiled roofs; **thatched roofs** rural; timber-frame on historical buildings
Signpost with the **pipe frame bent around the sign** = unique to Denmark
**Square B-type guardrails, no reflectors** — only a few European countries use this
Pedestrian sign **5 stripes**, first & fifth **touch the triangle edge**
Red-and-white **chevrons**, often low to the ground; unique **town-entry sign** (red slash when leaving)
Street-level poles **rare** → mostly big **overhead power lines** near highways; bicycle paths common
**PostNord** postal boxes; gas brands Ingo / OK / Uno-X / YX (each shared with one neighbour)
Road numbering runs **east→west**; roads 2–5 grouped by region; **Bornholm only has 1-roads**
**6 regional bus companies**, each with its own colour scheme + bus-stop sign
**Bornholm**: green/red/white bus stops + green bicycle signs
**Læsø** island: traditional **seaweed (eelgrass) roofs**
**Anholt** island: only **Gen2**, gloomy overcast, sandy dirt on the roadside
**Short** white plate + EU blue strip (Åland = short, fully white, no strip)
Outer lines **always solid white** (Sweden/Norway use dashes); middle lines varied — yellow lines being **phased out**
Bollard: long black-and-white, **white rectangle reflector front + two dots back**, **diagonal** black strip
Snow poles orange w/ a **thin white band near the top** (single band; Norway/Sweden = wider, lower, multiple)
Finnish looks **unique**: long words, **double vowels**, letters **ä ö** (Estonian similar but adds õ ü)
Street names end **-tie / -katu**; white street sign, black text
Road signs **yellow background**; red-border signs have a **thin yellow stroke** on the edge (unique to Finland)
Pedestrian signs have **5 stripes** (Sweden/Norway usually 4)
Directional signs = **solid blue bg, white text + arrows**; chevrons black w/ yellow arrows
Wooden poles w/ streetlight, **lamp double-bolted at the top** (Sweden double too but lower & angled)
Signposts often have **blue-and-white striped** markings
Dense **boreal forest** (pines + white-trunk **birches**); **Land of a Thousand Lakes** — water everywhere
Much Gen3 shot in **autumn** → distinct fall colours = good Finland tiebreaker vs other Nordics
Architecture modern/utilitarian (concrete apartments downtown); wooden-clad suburbs, some dark-red painted
**K-Market & S-Market** supermarkets operate only in Finland (recognisable logos)
'Shitcam' (Ari) low-quality camera: wide circular blur, **no sky halo**, brownish tint — Finland (& Spain) have the most
Regional roads **3–4 digit, first digit = region**, distributed **south→north** (1 south … 9 north)
**Swedish bilingual** signs on the south/west coast → don't guess inland; **Sámi** bilingual far north (alphabet uses **c**)
**Wind turbines** & most farmland concentrated along the **west/south coast**
**Lapland**: snow poles with **2 long reflectors** (2023+ coverage); small stunted spindly trees in the north
**Åland Islands**: monolingually **Swedish**, **red-tinted** pavement, **dashed** outer lines, short all-white plates
City transit: **Helsinki** green-yellow trams + blue/white buses; **Tampere** red trams ('NYSSE' buses); **Turku** yellow buses; **Jyväskylä** green; **Oulu** pink front
Plates: long white, **blue strips both sides** (left more visible) — Italy has equal strips + shorter front plate
French: é à è â ê î ô û, œ, ç; **-ux** suffix only French; street = **rue**
**French bollard**: round white, **pointed top**, reflector band all around (Scotland's is blunt-topped)
**Waffle/ladder poles** (step indents; also Spain) + small **blue plates** on poles (France-unique) + diamond "French poletops"
**Yellow "D" road-number signs** = France only; town-entry signs red border w/ thin white outline
Chevrons **white-on-blue** (only France + Spain; Spain uses only 2 or 4 arrows)
Middle-line dashes = **two offset blocks** → France; blue house numbers (Italy rarely blue)
Peugeot / Renault / Citroën ≈ half of all cars; smallcam (most of W. Europe)
**Postal code first 2 digits = department number** (alphabetical); also inside the right blue plate strip
Phone: **01 = Île-de-France**, 02–05 run clockwise from the NW
**Department logos** on bus stops / km markers / signbacks → identify department
Stone houses, gable roofs w/ orange tiles (S) or black slate (N); very diverse regional architecture
Regional languages appear (Breton NW, Alsatian E, Occitan/Basque/Catalan S)
All car coverage = **Gen4**; **bollard roughly every 50 m** (B&W, white/grey reflectors, orange at intersections)
German: **Ä Ö Ü ß**; Luxembourg has near-identical bollards but **3 bolts** (Germany = 2)
**Yellow town-entry signs** (black text, metal frame) = town / municipality / county
**"Einbahnstraße"** one-way sign = Germany (Austria = "EINBAHN"); yellow mailboxes
km markers: **blue = Autobahn (A-road)**, white w/ black border = B/local; black/dark **sign backs** (also IT/RO/AL)
**Wind turbines** far more common than anywhere else in Europe; green windshield sticker ("4")
Elevation: NW very flat · centre hilly · **south = Alps** (Austrian border)
**Postal codes counter-clockwise: 0 = Saxony (E) → 9 = N. Bavaria**
Place-name suffixes: **-ow / -itz = east** (Slavic); **-weiler / -ach = south**
"Commie blocks" (socialist-era apartments) → former **East** Germany
Brick houses → NW; thatched roofs + rock walls → Schleswig-Holstein / N. Frisian islands; vineyards → SW
White **3-section road markers** + round wooden bollards; black-disk bollard bases → all of eastern Germany
Roadside post styles differ by state (rocky unpainted → Rhineland-Palatinate/Saxony; tall white → Saarland/Thuringia)
Mostly **"shitcam"** (brownish colours, big circular blur); Gen4 = smallcam; drives on the **left**
Plates: long, usually **white**; commercial = **yellow**, EV = **green**
**B&W striped signposts** (also BD/NP/LK); poles almost always concrete/metal (wood v. rare); common = square concrete pole w/ trident top
English on much signage; many regional **scripts** (best region tool → below)
**Devanagari** (bar across top of words) = Hindi → central/north; Marathi (has ळ) → Maharashtra
**Gujarati** = like Hindi but **no top bar** → Gujarat
**Bengali** (left-facing triangles) → West Bengal/NE (& Bangladesh); Assamese variant (ৰ ৱ) → Assam
**Kannada** (curves + small circles) → Karnataka; **Telugu** (checkmark diacritics on top) → Telangana/Andhra
**Tamil** (curves + 90° angles) → Tamil Nadu (& Sri Lanka); **Malayalam** (upside-down U shapes) → Kerala
**Oriya** (round "skull-emoji" tops) → Odisha; **Punjabi** (broken top bar, soft letters) → Punjab
Meghalaya = almost exclusively **English**
**Holey concrete poles** = Gujarat; yellow tuk-tuks w/ black roofs → south (TG/AP/TN)
Pole paint: **5+ thin** black/yellow stripes → Delhi; **exactly 4 thick** → Bihar
Gurdwaras (white domes, Khanda symbol) → Punjab; bright flat orange ridged roof tiles → Tamil Nadu
Plates **black w/ white letters** (3 white sections through blur); commercial = yellow; newer = white (Malaysia = 2 sections)
Drives on the **left**; **motorcycles have front plates** (unique in SE Asia)
**Red-and-white banners** everywhere; cigarette ads (word **PERINGATAN**, ~18,000 Rp)
Yellow **waystones** angled toward road w/ cutout naming 3 towns (Philippines = perpendicular, text away)
Language Dutch-influenced: **televisi** (Malay = televisyen); currency Rupiah (Rp)
**No coverage on Papua**; sparse Kalimantan/Aceh/Central Sulawesi
**Kabupaten (Kab.)** regency names on signs → pinpoint region (learnable)
Postal codes: **1xxxx = Jakarta → 9xxxx = Maluku/Papua** (west→east)
Satellite-dish angle → which side of equator + how far (points to W. Kalimantan)
Oil palm → Sumatra/Kalimantan; rice → Java; Nusa (Lesser Sunda) drier & shrubbier
Crown-like roof pinnacles, Hindu temples, dark andesite stone; blue horizontal pole text
Cardinal words in addresses: Utara(N) Timur(E) Selatan(S) Barat(W) Tengah(C)
Red soil w/ white-soil layer spilling on road → Bangka Belitung islands
Plate: long **white** (incl. back) + blue strip left — **white backplate** distinguishes from UK (yellow back)
**Yellow dashed outer road lines** — common in Ireland, ~never in UK (also NZ)
**Only European country with yellow diamond warning signs** (regulatory signs stay red/white)
Signage **bilingual English + Irish** (Irish in italics)
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (UK = white-on-black); yield says **"YIELD"** (UK = "GIVE WAY")
Pole sticker: lightning bolt but **no man being zapped** (UK sticker has the man)
Green+white bollards; rural gates **rounded corners** (UK square; but N. Ireland also rounded)
"Boreens" — tiny rural roads with a **strip of grass down the middle**; drives on the left; smallcam
Cream-white buildings w/ black roofs, sometimes grey corner stones
Regional roads = 3-digit; **first digit = region**, numbered **anti-clockwise from Dublin**
Phone area codes grouped by first digit (learnable) → region
West coast = rugged hilly moorland, brownish/yellow grass, exposed rock (vs neat green fields of the east)
**EV Gen4 car** (grey back w/ black semicircle) covers most of the east & parts of NW
Distinctive flat **tabletop mountains** → north Sligo / Leitrim; hilly tiny Gen4 roads → Donegal
Plate: two blue strips + **short front plate** (unique; Albania has 2 strips but not short)
**Triangular** bollard, white w/ black top, red front / white rear (Albania identical)
Road lines often = white outer + **no middle line**; **double guardrails** common w/ red reflectors (Spain = yellow)
Italian: double consonants, lots of i's, words end in vowels; street = **via**
Round concrete poles (unusual for the Med) + concrete trident poletops; black/dark sign backs
Directional signs **blue w/ white arrow, ALL CAPS**; Autostrade (motorway) signs green
km markers: white square, **Roman numeral (100 m) over Arabic (km)**; "passo carrabile" no-parking signs
Road codes **SS / SR / SP** = State / Regional / Provincial; chevrons white-on-black
Pastel stone houses w/ terracotta roofs; **Tuscany** = irregular brown-grey brick
Area-code first digit generally increases **east then south**
**German** bilingual signs (German majority) → South Tyrol / Alto Adige
**French** bilingual signs ("rue") → Aosta Valley in the NW Alps
**Po Valley** = dead flat + hazy air (north); Apulia (Bari) = olive groves + red dirt + rock walls (SE)
Sicily = arid hilly + rocky mtns; Sardinia = grey rocks poking up (NE); cacti → far south
**Trulli** (conical stone roofs) → Itria Valley / Alberobello; Apulian flat-roof light houses
Volcanoes as landmarks: **Etna** → E Sicily, **Vesuvius** → Naples; **Venice** = canals everywhere
**Slovene** bilingual signs near the Slovenian border (Friuli / Trieste)
**Low-cam** (camera looks down, road looks wide) — only other always-low-cam = Switzerland (blurred there)
Drives **left**; writing = kanji + hiragana (loopy, soft) + katakana (geometric, loan words)
Plates: short white w/ **green text**; Kei cars = **yellow w/ black text**
Round concrete poles w/ screw-like extensions + info plates (region key); road lines **never yellow on the outside**
Prefecture roads = **hexagonal shields** (kanji under the number); distinctive red inverted-triangle stop sign; traffic mirrors; rural retaining walls
**Area codes increase toward the south**
Learn place-name kanji: 県 (prefecture), 市 (city), 北/南/東/西 (N/S/E/W) → fast city finding
4 main islands N→S: Hokkaidō, Honshū, Shikoku, Kyūshū; 47 prefectures
Pole plates, reflectors, guy-wires & transformer mounting styles differ per region (Hokkaidō, Tōhoku, Kantō, Kansai…) — strong pinpoint tools
Orange arrow above top pole plate → Chūgoku; orange/black pole bands → Shikoku
Hokkaidō = wide flat grid farmland, snow; subtropical dense green → Kyūshū/Okinawa
Bollards may have the city/prefecture written in kanji → near-pinpoint
**Octagonal concrete poles** (also Colombia); short plates, front plate required
Small **white cylindrical bollards w/ black base** = Mexico-unique; white outer + single yellow middle lines
Stop sign **"ALTO"** (S. America + PR = "PARE"; also PA/CR/GT use ALTO)
Cacti everywhere; thick arrows on signs; yellow accents on kerbs; rooftop cylindrical water tanks
**OXXO** & **Pemex** stations; Nahuatl place names (lots of x, tl, -tlan, -ec)
Has **Gen2** (rare in Latin America); Gen3 white car w/ stubby coiled antenna; US pickups common
Area-code 1st digit = region; postal codes ordered alphabetically by state name
**Blue agave fields → Jalisco/Nayarit** (tequila); Mexican Giant Cardon cactus → Baja California
Saguaro cactus → Sonora; **boojum trees** (tentacle-like) → southern Baja California
Monterrey = tall mountains ringing the city; blinding **white soil → Yucatán** (Quintana Roo)
Red brick buildings → states west of CDMX (Jalisco, Michoacán, Guanajuato)
**Jalisco bollards** have a crown + "Jalisco" text (only Mexican bollard with writing)
Bollards w/ red band near top → Nayarit; thick cross on sign backs → Chihuahua
Plates long **yellow** + blue strip left (Europe yellow = only NL, Luxembourg, UK-back)
**Essentially no street-level utility poles** (Belgium has them); extraordinarily **flat** land
Water everywhere: canals, ditches, dikes, bridges; windmills (old & wind turbines)
**Bike paths everywhere** (reddish-pink lanes) + tons of cyclists; brick roads common
Brick **rumble strip** on rural road edges (~unique); speed bumps w/ long/short white stripes
Dutch: double vowels, ij/oe, "IJ" one letter; street = straat / -weg / -laan / -plein; signs blue w/ white text
Road markers **green**: **A-road = red box** (highway), **N-road = yellow box** (regional); sign edges wrap to the back
Row houses ≈ half of housing (very high); red-brown brick, orange/grey tiles
Landline area codes (3–4 digit) are geographically grouped → region
N-road **hectometre markers** have province-specific designs → identify province
**Frisian** language on some signs (vowels w/ circumflex Â Ê Ô Û) → Friesland (north)
**Low rolling hills** (rare for NL) → Southern Limburg
West & north are **below sea level** → polder landscape (grid ditches); dunes line the North Sea coast
Very **mountainous, fjords** along most of the coast (Finland/Denmark flat; Sweden mixed)
Mostly **wooden houses**; many **red or black-painted**; Norway has more **white** houses & fewer brick than SE/FI
Long white plate + EU strip; **green commercial plates** common on vans (green plates most-likely Norway in Europe)
Letters **Æ Ø Å** (Danish also; Swedish only Å); more E's than Swedish; Norwegian **kk** vs Swedish **ck**
Small roads = **long dashed white outer lines**; big roads solid + **orange-tinged yellow** middle; lines often dotted
Bollard: thin curved rectangle, reflector inside a **black parallelogram**
**Snow poles orange**, thin white reflector **mid-pole** (Sweden wider/higher; Finland at very top); wooden ones w/ painted reflectors
Guardrails often on **wooden posts** (not common in SE/FI)
Pedestrian sign: **4 stripes**, simply-drawn person (Sweden detailed no hat; Finland 5 stripes)
Road signs = **red outline + white background** (Sweden & Finland = yellow); chevrons yellow-on-black
Directional signs **yellow + black border**; road # in green box (Euro/major) or white (minor); blue **M** signs on small roads
Wooden poles w/ small **black metal cap**; lamp mounted w/ **single bolt** (Sweden/Finland double)
Traditional houses often have **grass-covered roofs** (far rarer in SE/FI)
Street suffixes **-veien / -gata / -gate**; street signs **centred on top** of the post (Sweden = side/lamp)
Road number **first digit = region**; small white signs show road # (prefix **R** = findable, **F**/Fv = hard) + distance
**Sámi bilingual signs** in the north — letters **Á Đ** and many vowels in a row
Deep **fjords + tall mountains** between Bergen–Trondheim; sharp rocky treeless peaks on **Lofoten**
Farmlands in 3 zones: **SE/Oslo** (flat, rolling forested hills), **Trondheim** (steep hilly), **Stavanger** (vast open, round rock walls, grass)
**Black snow poles** most common between Bergen and Trondheim
**Oslo**: fairly large black or red pole plates; SE Norway = small wide black plates
**Stavanger**: two **red stripes** around lamp posts
**Bergen**: distinctive green **fire hydrants**
**Russian border** (Kirkenes, E6/E105): humid green foggy road, thin birches/pines; Russian text on signs
**Røros**: densely-built traditional wooden mining town
Plate: long white + blue strip left (EV = green)
Bollard: **red strip wraps all the way around** (red front / white back reflector)
**Holey poles** (thin concrete, see-through holes NOT reaching the bottom) — HU/RO ones are wider & holes reach bottom
**Warning signs = yellow background + thin red border** (unique in Europe to Poland)
Pedestrian sign = **single horizontal line** (Austria/Belgium use two); chevrons white w/ red arrows
All-white lines, **double middle lines common**; B-type guardrails w/ round red reflectors
Polish: **Ł** (unique), Ń, Ż, Ą/Ę; consonant-heavy, uses **W not V**; direction signs green w/ distinct font
Flat & green, tree-lined roads; very Catholic → roadside shrines & Polish Catholic flags
Regional roads 3-digit, **first digit = region** (yellow boxes); landline area codes 2-digit, well-clustered
Place names: **-owo → north, -ów → south** (Kraków is southern), -ino/-yno → NW
South border = hilly/mountainous (Tatras); rest flat; wooden houses → east (Podlaskie)
Minority languages: German → Opole; Kashubian → north; Lithuanian → Puńsk
Plates **fully white, black text** (Europe usu. has blue strip)
Cyrillic w/ **Ы Э Ё Ъ**; улица = street (ул.), город = city (г.)
Many bollards + unusually **wide intersections**
Square concrete poles (also post-Soviet states); concrete support blocks at pole base
B&W striped guardrails / kerbs / pole shields
Gen3: black or white car w/ **long antenna**; pedestrian crossings = alternating white+yellow stripes
Birch everywhere; **larch** more common further east (Siberia)
Kamchatka = grey volcanic soil + volcanoes; Caucasus mtns → south (Dagestan dry/flat)
RHD Japanese cars more common **east of Novosibirsk**
Area codes: **8-** = west, **3-** = central, **4-** = east/Moscow
Gen3 unblurred plate regional code (right side): 01 = Adygea … learnable list
Omsk = birch patches in green farm fields (summer Gen4); Kaliningrad = white-striped lined oaks
Bilingual signs (Tatar, Bashkir…) → Tatarstan / Bashkortostan
Plate: long white, single **blue strip left** (Portugal = yellow strip right; Italy = two blue strips)
Bollard: yellow-orange front reflector + **two white dots on the back**, hollow
**Hunting signs** ("coto…caza", black/white diagonal split) = Spain-unique; lots of high-voltage pylons
Pedestrian crossing = **8 stripes** (most in Europe; else only Andorra)
Red sign borders run **to the edge** (France/Italy/Portugal keep a white edge); flat signposts
Ladder poles + French poletops; chevrons black/white or **blue/white** (blue = only Spain + France)
Spanish: **Ñ** (vs Portuguese ç/lh/-ão); street = **calle**; garage no-parking = "Vado permanente"
Rural roads may have **dashed outer lines** (helps vs Portugal/Italy where they're absent)
**Regional road codes** on markers (SG = Segovia, etc.) → pinpoint province
Area codes: 2 digits → very accurate; all landlines start with **9**
**Catalan** (à, ç, "i" for and, street = carrer, "Gual permanent") → Catalonia / Valencia / Balearics
Big **square chevrons** with a single arrow common in Catalonia
**Basque** (x/k/tx heavy, non-Romance; street = kalea) → Basque Country & Navarre
**Galician** (like Portuguese but uses ñ; street = **Rúa**, municipality = concello) → NW
Unique granaries **"hórreos"** (raised stone/wood); plain concrete-block buildings
**Yellow snow poles** with 1–2 reflectors are unique to Asturias
Strip of **semi-desert** cutting Navarra→Aragón (white rocky soil; red soil in Teruel)
**White villages** on mountain faces; vast olive plantations; Sierra Nevada snow-capped Nov–May
Diverse: dry Mediterranean plateaus & hills common; lush green Atlantic → far N (Galicia/Asturias)
**Canary Islands**: arid/volcanic (Lanzarote moon-like black soil); own bollards
**Ceuta & Melilla** (enclaves in N. Africa): black-and-white guardrails are unique to Ceuta
Swedish: **Å Ä Ö** (Danish/Norwegian have only Å; Finnish has Ä Ö + long double-vowel words)
**All road lines white** (Norway & Finland use yellow); highway outer line split into sectioned pattern
Road signs = red border + **yellow background**; **chevrons = yellow arrows on blue** (only Sweden in Europe)
Lots of **red+yellow** infrastructure (roadwork, signpost stripes) — distinctive to Sweden
Bollards black+white (orange reflector at intersections); wooden poles w/ small **black cap on top** (Nordic)
Pedestrian sign **4 stripes** (Finland/Denmark = 5); blue signs with a large white **M** on small roads
Street suffixes **vägen / gatan** (v / g); dark-red painted wooden countryside houses (Nordic)
**Volvo ≈ 30% of cars**; Swedish church flag (yellow, red cross, crown) near churches
Gen4 cars: red / white / blue (only the blue one appears in NO/FI/DK); Gen3 white car long antenna
North & west = mountainous w/ long narrow lakes; south & east = flatter; boreal forest + many lakes
Snow poles orange w/ reflector above middle (Finland's is thinner, near the top)
Plate: long white + blue strip left (like most of Europe)
Turkish: **Ç Ş Ö Ü Ğ** + **dotless ı** (essentially unique) & dotted **İ**
Bollard: thin **rectangular plain white**, red front reflector (rare yellow in snowy areas)
Road lines inconsistent (white, yellow, or mixed); grainy **"salt-and-pepper" pavement** common
**Divided highways extremely common** (vs other Mediterranean countries)
Chevrons usually **red-on-white** (arrow tip doesn't touch the edge); black/yellow multi-arrow at intersections
Stop sign says **"DUR"** (unique to Turkey); direction signs have very wide letter spacing
Snow poles = **black-orange-black-white** sequence w/ small red reflectors
Uses **both A- and B-type guardrails** (seeing both excludes most of S. Europe & Middle East)
**Metal ladder or mesh poles** extremely common
Gen3 = no antenna; Gen4 white car; grey **pickup truck** in some Gen4 (also Georgia); smallcam exists
Tall square **apartment blocks**; Muslim-majority → **mosques w/ tall minarets** in most towns
Area codes **1st digit 2 (west) → 4 (east)**; km markers show **province code (first 2 digits, ~alphabetical 01–76)**
North = lush/forested; **NE = deep-green grassy round hills** (Gen3), few trees; SE arid; SW Mediterranean
Roofs: tiled → western ⅔; flat → SE + south coast; metal/low-pyramid → far east (Lake Van)
**Göreme/Cappadocia** (Nevşehir): sandstone monoliths w/ carved dwellings; **Mt. Ararat** volcano far east; whitewashed flat-roof houses → Bodrum
Plates: **white front, yellow back** (Ireland = white both + always a blue strip); drives **left**
Middle line = long dashes with **cat's-eye reflectors** in the gaps; double-yellow no-stopping lines at kerbs
UK bollard: rounded, red-front/white-back reflector, black base & top w/ white stripe
Chevrons **black w/ white arrows** (Ireland = yellow arrows); **triangular** red warning signs (Ireland = diamond yellow)
**"GIVE WAY"** yield (Ireland = "YIELD"); pole warning sticker shows a **man being zapped** (Ireland = no man)
Roads: **A / M** = major, **B** = minor; terraced / semi-detached houses (61%); brick + chimney pots
A/B-road **first digit = zone** (grouped, like a pie); NI phone = **028**, London = **020**
Bilingual English/Welsh signs; road marking **"Araf"** (= Slow) is Wales-only; Welsh uses LL, W, Y
Scottish Gaelic (green/yellow text) north of Glasgow; barren mountainous **Highlands**; 'Passing Place' diamond signs; red+white round bollards
Stone buildings: granite → Aberdeen, red sandstone → Glasgow/Dumfries, yellow sandstone → Edinburgh
England countryside = green, hedgerows, low rolling hills (Ireland looks similar — check other clues)
Warning stickers wrapped all the way around the pole → Northern Ireland; Cornish palms → far SW coast
Isle of Man = **Gen2 only**, counts as UK for country streaks
Speed signs say **"SPEED LIMIT"** (Canada = "MAXIMUM"); **miles**, feet/inches; limits often end in 5
Wordy signs (railway crossing spelled out); **US English** (center, color)
**"No Passing Zone"** yellow triangle = US only; rectangular junction signs (Canada = diamond)
Yellow centre + white outer lines; **double yellow** common (v. rare in Europe)
White+orange **fibre-optic markers** ~unique to US; rumble strips common
Trailer/mobile homes rural (esp. SE); **Baptist churches** common
US+Canada: post mailbox w/ flag; wooden poles + **cylindrical transformers**; short plates (no blue strip); overhead signal bars; long-nose semis (Europe = cabover)
Gen2 rare → if Gen2 in N. America likely **Canada** (except Alaska, some Hawaii); Gen3 mirror = **single peak** (Canada = two peaks); NA Gen3 usually **no antenna**
East of Missouri R. = denser pop. & greener; West = mountains, drier
Deep South (OK→SC) **reddish soil**; SW Four Corners red mesas/tablelands
PNW (WA/OR/N.CA coast) **temperate rainforest**; Great Plains flat, few trees
New England architecture (shingle/wood panel, porch, high roof) + **three-deckers** → Northeast
Interstates: **even = W–E** (low #s at Gulf/Mexico), **odd = S–N** (low #s at West Coast); US Highways numbered inversely
~half of states have **no front plate**; each state has a unique plate design (some readable through blur)
3-digit **area codes** (not clustered but learnable); each state has its own **flag** (distinct ones appear often)
Appalachians (low forested mtns) → east; Rockies → MT–NM; Great Basin (mtns + dry shrubby plains) → NV/UT
**Saguaro cacti** (tall tubes w/ branching arms) → southern Arizona, Sonoran border
Utility poles w/ **3 yellow stripes** at the base (most of CA, exc. Modoc/Lassen NE)
**Front Range**: a N–S mountain ridge flanking Denver/Boulder/Colorado Springs, plains to the east
Central FL (Orlando↔Lakeland) = the only real **rolling hills**, lakes + groves; jungle-straight roads far south
**Square-reflector bollard** (also UT/AZ) — Idaho appears with both 1 and 2 bolts
**Concrete paving** on ordinary rural roads much more common than elsewhere (ignore bridges/highways/cities)
**White reflectors** on rural signposts (esp. stop signs) — unique to Kansas
Uniquely-shaped **rumble strips**: thin, long recessed lines on the very outside road edges
**Shotgun houses** (very narrow 1-storey wood) → New Orleans area (also Houston, Louisville, Mississippi)
Stoplights & signs **hang from wires** across big intersections, often in yellow boxes
**Yellow signs w/ vertical black triangles** before bridges at major river crossings & guardrail ends
County highways use a **letter system (A–ZZZ)** — Missouri signs lack the word 'county' (Wisconsin has it)
**Sandhills**: rolling grass-covered dunes in the central-north of the state
Bollard = **white vertical-rectangle reflector w/ black outline** on a thin stick
White metal **"E-2" sign** beside the road, typically at bridge ends
Bollard with **3 circular reflectors**; Pueblo-Revival adobe (flat roofs, rounded, sand-brown) buildings
**Square guardrails** (long top bar on square legs) dominant here (also Wyoming)
Street signs showing **street numbers + arrows** alongside the name
**Green sign, 3 rows of numbers** on the back of rural stop signs at intersections
Speed signs often say just **"SPEED"** (not "SPEED LIMIT"); bollard = very long rectangular reflector on top
White intersection signs w/ the **converging state-route number** on top; **keystone** route markers
**White diamond reflector** bollard, usually **2 bolts** (Montana = 1 bolt); Black Hills (conifers) far west
**Yellow/red bands** ('ketchup & mustard') around signposts; grainy salt-and-pepper roads; Hill Country oaks W of Austin
Bollard = **white square reflector atop a black rectangle** (unique); streets named like **"600 South"**
Small **green signs, 3 rows of numbers**, 2nd row starts with **0** (New York's starts with a NYSDOT district #)
Bollard = white/brown post, **white rectangle on front + two small squares on back**; golden wheat fields east
Street signs with a **fraction-in-a-circle** symbol
Simple **white bollards w/ black reflectors** (unique); green **ATV Route** signs; orange rectangle+diamond pole plate (also MI UP)
Bollard: dark post, white circular reflector w/ post rising **above** it; top sometimes painted **white/grey** (unique)
**Interstate 19** (Tucson → Mexico) is the only US highway signed in **kilometres** (speed still MPH)
**Ford Bronco** (unblurred black roof, red hood) on unpaved roads = TransAmerica Trail promo, OR→NC
**Devil's Tower** (lone butte, NE Wyoming) visible for miles; Badlands/Theodore Roosevelt = sand-brown eroded rock
